items.q: How do you see a non-luminous object like a book?
items.correct: Light from a source reflects off the book and enters your eyes.
items.misconception: Your eyes send out beams of vision that hit the book and let you see it.
items.random_wrong: The book emits a special invisible signal that your brain detects directly.
items.why: The 'extramission' model of vision is a stubborn childhood intuition, reinforced by phrases like 'cast your eye over' or comic-book vision rays.

items.q: If one EM wave has a higher frequency than another, what is true about its wavelength?
items.correct: It is shorter — wavelength and frequency are inversely related (c = f x lambda).
items.misconception: It is also longer, because both increase together.
items.random_wrong: It is unchanged — only the speed differs.
items.why: Students treat all wave properties as moving together (more of one = more of the other), missing the fixed-speed constraint that ties them inversely.

items.q: Is ultraviolet light visible to humans?
items.correct: No — even though it sits next to violet on the spectrum, its frequency is above the range our eyes detect.
items.misconception: Yes — UV is visible because it's right next to violet.
items.random_wrong: Yes — UV is the colour purple.
items.why: Students assume adjacency on the EM spectrum implies visibility; they don't appreciate the sharp biological cut-off at around 400 nm.

items.q: Which colour of visible light has the LONGEST wavelength?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Red. “Violet” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A red T-shirt is lit by green light only. What does it look like?
items.why: Correct answer: Black. “Red” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many primary colours of LIGHT combine to make white light?
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
